# US Cement Tariffs Add $5-$10/Ton to Import Costs; Supply Gaps Emerge in Northern States

## Key Details
- **25% tariff** on cement imports from Canada and Mexico effective April 5, 2025
- **Subsequent adjustment**: Reduced to 10% across-the-board; Canada and Mexico exempted from certain tariffs
- **China tariff**: Increased to 145%
- **10% time-limited tariff**: 150 days, set to expire July 2026 (does not affect Canada/Mexico or existing tariffs)
- **U.S. imports ~20%** of total cement consumption
- **Canada and Mexico supply 27%** of U.S. cement imports (~7% of total U.S. consumption)
- **2023 import volumes**: 5 million metric tons from Canada; 2 million metric tons from Mexico
- **Cost impact**: Tariffs add $5-$10/ton for importers; overall market cost increase of ~1.86%
- **Regional impact**: Northern states (New York, Washington) hardest hit; Canadian imports account for up to 36% of regional consumption
- **Construction cost impact**: Aggregate construction costs estimated to rise ~8% under current tariff conditions
- **Material-specific tariff impact**: 5-25% depending on material type

## Analysis
- Domestic cement producers (CRH, Martin Marietta's remaining cement operations) benefit from tariff-driven import substitution
- CEMEX is uniquely positioned as both a domestic U.S. producer and a Mexican exporter, creating complex dynamics for its business
- Taiheiyo Cement (via CalPortland) could benefit as a domestic U.S. producer, though its Japanese parent may face challenges with the China tariff regime
- The 145% tariff on Chinese cement effectively eliminates that import source, tightening supply further
- Northern states face the most acute supply pressure, potentially driving regional price increases well above the 1.86% national average
- The expiration of the 10% time-limited tariff in July 2026 could create a brief price relief window
- Long-term, tariffs accelerate the trend toward domestic cement capacity investment, benefiting companies with existing U.S. plants
articles.title: US Cement Tariffs Add $5-$10/Ton to Import Costs; Supply Gaps Emerge in Northern States

# BEA GDP Q4 2025 Third Estimate: Scheduled for April 9, 2026

## Key Data Points
- **Release date:** April 9, 2026 at 8:30 a.m. EDT
- Originally scheduled for March 27 but delayed due to government data disruptions
- Will include: GDP (Third Estimate), Industries, Corporate Profits, State GDP, and State Personal Income
- This is the final estimate for Q4 2025 GDP

## Prior Estimates
- **Advance estimate** (January 30): **1.4%** annualized growth
- **Second estimate** (March 13): **0.7%** annualized growth (revised down 0.7 ppt)
- The second estimate was the weakest quarterly growth since early 2022
- Q3 2025 had been **4.4%**, making the Q4 slowdown dramatic

## Market Expectations
- Consensus expects the third estimate to confirm growth near **0.7-0.9%**
- Further downward revision would increase recession probability estimates
- Treasury yields fell on the second estimate, briefly supporting lower mortgage rates
- Fed futures market increased probability of a June 2026 rate cut after the second estimate

## Construction Industry Implications
- Weak Q4 GDP confirms the demand slowdown hitting construction activity
- Sub-1% growth typically correlates with declining construction starts within 2-3 quarters
- Corporate profits data (included in this release) will show building materials sector profitability trends
- State GDP breakdowns will reveal regional construction demand patterns
- Combined with Atlanta Fed GDPNow at 1.6% for Q1, the economy appears in a sustained low-growth trajectory

articles.content:
# Trump Administration Resets Section 232 Tariffs: 50% on Steel, Tiered System on Derivatives

## Key Details
- **Effective Date**: April 6, 2026
- **Primary Metal Articles** (steel, aluminum, copper): **50% tariff on full customs value**
- **Metal-Heavy Derivatives**: **25% tariff on full value**
- **Industrial/Electrical Grid Equipment**: **15% transitional rate** through 2027
- **Critical Change**: Tariffs now apply to full customs value of all covered articles, not just metal content
- **Previous System**: Based on metal-content valuation, which allowed lower duty exposure
- **Impact on Imports**: Foreign import share of U.S. finished steel market dropped from ~25% to ~14%
- **Imports Down**: 40% year-over-year decline in steel imports
- **New Product Categories**: Department of Commerce added 407 product categories to steel/aluminum tariffs
- **Domestic Price Effect**: HRC steel prices reached $1,074/short ton in early April 2026
- **Winners**: Nucor, Steel Dynamics, Cleveland-Cliffs transitioned from "price-takers to price-makers"
- **Downstream Impact**: Appliance, automotive, and construction manufacturers face sudden cost spikes

## Analysis
- The shift to full-value assessment is the most significant structural change to Section 232 since the tariffs were first imposed, as it closes loopholes where importers could minimize duties by undervaluing metal content
- With imports down 40% and domestic mills controlling pricing, HRC steel above $1,000/ton represents a structural floor that benefits all domestic producers
- For building materials companies that consume steel (HVAC, structural steel, metal roofing), these tariffs represent a significant cost headwind that will likely be passed through to construction costs
- The tiered system (50%/25%/15%) creates a cascading tariff structure that protects the entire steel value chain, from raw steel to semi-finished products
- This policy environment is the most favorable for domestic steel producers since the original Section 232 tariffs were imposed in 2018
articles.title: Trump Administration Resets Section 232 Tariffs: 50% on Steel, Tiered System on Derivatives

meta.source: White House / Customs Intel / Construction Dive

articles.content:
# April 6 Tariff Recalibration: New Rules for Steel, Aluminum, and Copper Items

## Key Details
- Effective **April 6, 2026**: updated Section 232 tariff rules
- Goods made **almost entirely of aluminum, steel, or copper** now face **50% tariff on the full item value**
- Previously, some finished/semi-finished products avoided the full tariff rate
- This closes loopholes where finished products with high metal content were classified outside Section 232
- Core tariff rates remain: **50%** on steel, aluminum, and copper (UK steel at 25%)

## What Changed
- **Annex I-A** maintains 50% duty on core commodities (coils, sheets, bars)
- New rules expand coverage to **downstream products**: finished metal items, fabricated components, assembled goods
- This catches construction products that are primarily metal but were previously imported as "finished goods" at lower tariff rates
- Examples: metal building components, prefabricated steel structures, aluminum curtain wall systems, copper electrical assemblies

## Construction Industry Impact
- The expanded tariff coverage means **more construction products** will be subject to the 50% rate
- Previously, some fabricated steel and aluminum building components were imported at lower rates
- Metal building manufacturers and facade contractors will see imported component costs rise
- Electrical equipment (switchgear, panels, transformers) made primarily of copper also affected

## Analysis
- This recalibration represents a further tightening of the tariff wall around the US construction supply chain
- It benefits domestic manufacturers of metal building products while raising costs for contractors who sourced fabricated components internationally
- For building materials: domestic metal building manufacturers, window/door producers, and electrical equipment makers gain competitive advantage; international sourcing becomes even more expensive
- The timing -- during the spring construction season -- means the cost impact will be felt immediately on active projects
- Combined with the Iran-driven energy cost shock, this creates a "double squeeze" on construction costs in Q2 2026
articles.title: April 6 Tariff Recalibration: New Rules for Steel, Aluminum, and Copper Items
